title: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect exterior intrusion include buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113.

title: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
option_for_consideration:
Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. The basic
types of sensors used for exterior intrusion are buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors. The IDS should be evenly distributed throughout critical areas to ensure uniform detection around the entire length of the facility’s perimeter. Sensors should be placed in locations that avoid electromagnetic, acoustic, thermal, nuclear radiation, optical, seismic, and meteorological interferences.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113, p. 286-287.

title: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility's exterior IDS does not include fence-associated sensors. Intrusion detection capabilities along the fence line would increase the security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: The facility's exterior IDS does not include fence-associated sensors. Intrusion detection capabilities along the fence line would increase the security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing fence-associated sensors for the exterior IDS. Options may include, but are not limited to, electric fields, sensors, and disturbance wires.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 99-100.

title: The exterior IDS lacks backup power. In the event of a power outage, the exterior IDS could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
vulnerability: The exterior IDS lacks backup power. In the event of a power outage, the exterior IDS could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of providing backup power to the IDS to ensure its continued operation during a loss of power to the facility.

title: The exterior IDS lacks backup power. In the event of a power outage, the exterior IDS could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
vulnerability: The exterior IDS lacks backup power. In the event of a power outage, the exterior IDS could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
option_for_consideration: Plan to increase security measures when the IDS is not operational, for example, increased roving patrols by the security force and/or additional access control measures.

title: The exterior IDS lacks tampering and system issue indicators. Without such indicators, tampering attempts and system issues will likely remain undetected by the facility.
vulnerability: The exterior IDS lacks tampering and system issue indicators. Without such indicators, tampering attempts and system issues will likely remain undetected by the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of incorporating tampering and system issue indicators to the IDS to ensure facility personnel are notified of incidents that could render the IDS nonoperational.

title: The detection zone is not kept clear of obstructions (e.g., dips, equipment, snow, ice, grass, debris).
vulnerability: The detection zone is not kept clear of obstructions (e.g., dips, equipment, snow, ice, grass, debris).
option_for_consideration: Establish a clear detection zone. Perimeter IDS systems perform more effectively when located in a defined clear zone. Such areas should be cleared of aboveground obstacles and vegetation that might block the IDS receivers and transmitters.

title: Facility or security force personnel do not assess exterior IDS alarms when activated.
vulnerability: Facility or security force personnel do not assess exterior IDS alarms when activated.
option_for_consideration: Implement policies and procedures for facility or security force personnel to assess alarms to evaluate the cause of activation. Timely alarm assessment is essential to effectively responding to intrusions.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 152; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 281-285; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 51-52.

title: The facility's exterior IDS lacks alarm enunciators to alert staff of alarm activation. Without visual or audible alarm indicators, attempted and successful exterior security breaches will likely remain unnoticed.
vulnerability: The facility's exterior IDS lacks alarm enunciators to alert staff of alarm activation. Without visual or audible alarm indicators, attempted and successful exterior security breaches will likely remain unnoticed.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing alarm enunciators for the IDS.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 111-112.

title: The facility's exterior IDS lacks alarm enunciators to alert staff of alarm activation. Without visual or audible alarm indicators, attempted and successful exterior security breaches will likely remain unnoticed.
vulnerability: The facility's exterior IDS lacks alarm enunciators to alert staff of alarm activation. Without visual or audible alarm indicators, attempted and successful exterior security breaches will likely remain unnoticed.
option_for_consideration: Contact the IDS manufacturer and/or installer to evaluate changes or upgrades that can be made to the system to allow for the incorporation of visual or audible alarm indicators.

title: The facility does not maintain the exterior IDS according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
vulnerability: The facility does not maintain the exterior IDS according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
option_for_consideration: Maintain the IDS according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacements for the IDS.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 156; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-97.

title: The facility does not test the exterior IDS periodically. Failure to periodically test the IDS could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
vulnerability: The facility does not test the exterior IDS periodically. Failure to periodically test the IDS could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
option_for_consideration: Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the IDS and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 154-156.

title: The facility does not have an interior IDS to detect attempted and successful security breaches.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an interior IDS to detect attempted and successful security breaches.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the weakest points of entry into the facility and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect interior intrusion include boundary penetration sensors, interior motion sensors, proximity sensors, door sensors, and window sensors.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.1.2, p. 5-34 to 5-38, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility's interior IDS does not include door sensors. There are critical areas within the facility that would benefit from increased capabilities for intrusion detection.
vulnerability: The facility's interior IDS does not include door sensors. There are critical areas within the facility that would benefit from increased capabilities for intrusion detection.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing door sensors for critical areas within the facility. Options may include, but are not limited to, glass breakage sensors, grid mesh, vibration sensors, magnetic contacts, and conducting tape.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 104-105; Norman, T.L., 2012, Electronic Access Control, Waltham, MA: Elsevier, p. 290; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 116-126.

title: The facility's interior IDS does not include window sensors. Intrusion detection capabilities for facility windows would increase the security posture of the building.
vulnerability: The facility's interior IDS does not include window sensors. Intrusion detection capabilities for facility windows would increase the security posture of the building.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing window sensors, at least for critical areas within the facility. Options may include, but are not limited to, glass breakage sensors, grid mesh, vibration sensors, balanced magnetic contacts, and conducting tape.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 104-105; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 116-126.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3, p. 5-44 to 5-50, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite. Site-specific factors must be considered when selecting components that comprise a particular CCTV system. For example, the size of the system, in terms of the number of cameras needed, is the minimum number required to view all electronic security system sensor detection fields. In addition, some cameras may require artificial light sources. Finally, performance criteria and physical, environmental, and economic considerations must be factored into the component selection.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3, p. 5-44 to 5-50, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Install CCTV cameras where they will capture an unobstructed field of view of the desired scene and where there is minimal risk of vandalism, sabotage, or environmental damage, if possible.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations outdoors: along site perimeter detection zone, at controlled access points, within the protected area, and to view approaches to critical assets.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations indoors: at selected assets within the protected area, and at critical portals adjacent to assets.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Provide for effective monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.

title: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
option_for_consideration: Determine the objectives of video recording and retention (e.g., verify information, prove it, or aid prosecution with it) to select the appropriate video recording and storage systems for the facility.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.3, p. 5-49, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: CCTV coverage of the facility's perimeter is limited.
vulnerability: CCTV coverage of the facility's perimeter is limited.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate CCTV coverage of the facility perimeter to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 149-150.

title: CCTV coverage of areas of concern, such as gates and entryways, is limited.
vulnerability: CCTV coverage of areas of concern, such as gates and entryways, is limited.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate CCTV coverage of areas of concern to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 149-150.

title: CCTV coverage of critical areas/SAAs is limited.
vulnerability: CCTV coverage of critical areas/SAAs is limited.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate CCTV coverage of critical areas/SAAs to determine if it meets the facility's security requirements. Explore options to increase coverage as necessary.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 149-150.

title: The facility's CCTV system lack backup power. In the event of a power outage, the CCTV system could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
vulnerability: The facility's CCTV system lack backup power. In the event of a power outage, the CCTV system could not continue to operate, reducing the facility's security capabilities.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of providing backup power to the CCTV system to ensure its continued operation during a loss of power to the facility.

title: The facility does not utilize video analytics or anomaly detection to automatically identify things of interest such as behavior or objects, without an operator having to view the video. This capability would increase the effectiveness of the CCTV system to detect abnormal or suspicious activity.
vulnerability: The facility does not utilize video analytics or anomaly detection to automatically identify things of interest such as behavior or objects, without an operator having to view the video. This capability would increase the effectiveness of the CCTV system to detect abnormal or suspicious activity.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of upgrading the CCTV system to include video analytics or anomaly detection software.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 134-135.

title: The CCTV system is monitored by trained, but not dedicated, security staff. Security staff dedicated to  the CCTV system could more effectively monitor the system for abnormal or suspicious activity.
vulnerability: The CCTV system is monitored by trained, but not dedicated, security staff. Security staff dedicated to  the CCTV system could more effectively monitor the system for abnormal or suspicious activity.
option_for_consideration: Provide for more effective monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

title: The CCTV system is monitored by trained, but not dedicated, security staff. Security staff dedicated to  the CCTV system could more effectively monitor the system for abnormal or suspicious activity.
vulnerability: The CCTV system is monitored by trained, but not dedicated, security staff. Security staff dedicated to  the CCTV system could more effectively monitor the system for abnormal or suspicious activity.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.

title: The facility does not use real-time monitoring for the CCTV system, which limits the facility to reviewing recorded material only.
vulnerability: The facility does not use real-time monitoring for the CCTV system, which limits the facility to reviewing recorded material only.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for real-time monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.

title: The facility does not use real-time monitoring for the CCTV system, which limits the facility to reviewing recorded material only.
vulnerability: The facility does not use real-time monitoring for the CCTV system, which limits the facility to reviewing recorded material only.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.

title: The CCTV video is not recorded; therefore, the facility cannot review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
vulnerability: The CCTV video is not recorded; therefore, the facility cannot review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to record CCTV video, and select video recording and storage systems for the facility as appropriate.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 148-149.

title: The CCTV video is not recorded; therefore, the facility cannot review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
vulnerability: The CCTV video is not recorded; therefore, the facility cannot review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
option_for_consideration: Upgrade the CCTV system to provide for video recording and storage. Develop a policy for the review of recorded information (e.g., periodically or only after an incident).

title: The CCTV video is recorded, but the footage is reviewed only after known incidents.
vulnerability: The CCTV video is recorded, but the footage is reviewed only after known incidents.
option_for_consideration: Review the information recorded by the CCTV system periodically, to establish awareness of all incidents captured on camera.

title: The CCTV video is recorded, but the footage is reviewed only after known incidents.
vulnerability: The CCTV video is recorded, but the footage is reviewed only after known incidents.
option_for_consideration: Routinely review information recorded by the CCTV system after-hours (on fast-forward).

title: The facility keeps recorded information from the CCTV system for more than 1 month; however, the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents could be enhanced by extending the storage duration for recorded information.
vulnerability: The facility keeps recorded information from the CCTV system for more than 1 month; however, the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents could be enhanced by extending the storage duration for recorded information.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to extend the time that recorded information from the CCTV system remains in storage. Explore options to increase the system's storage capacity as necessary.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 142; Patterson, D.G., 2013, Implementing Physical Protection Systems: A Practical Guide, Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 15-16, p. 125-126.

title: The facility does not keep recorded information from the CCTV system, which limits the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
vulnerability: The facility does not keep recorded information from the CCTV system, which limits the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to store recorded information from the CCTV system, and explore options to implement this capability as necessary.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 142; Patterson, D.G., 2013, Implementing Physical Protection Systems: A Practical Guide, Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 15-16, p. 125-126.

title: The facility does not keep recorded information from the CCTV system, which limits the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
vulnerability: The facility does not keep recorded information from the CCTV system, which limits the facility's ability to review footage to investigate or evaluate security incidents.
option_for_consideration: Upgrade the CCTV system to provide for video storage. Develop a policy for the review of recorded information (e.g., periodically or only after an incident).

title: The facility does not maintain the CCTV system according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
vulnerability: The facility does not maintain the CCTV system according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
option_for_consideration: Maintain the CCTV system according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacement for the IDS.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-97, p. 162-165.

title: The most recent update to the CCTV system was more than 5 years ago. Without regular updates, the overall effectiveness of the system may be degraded.
vulnerability: The most recent update to the CCTV system was more than 5 years ago. Without regular updates, the overall effectiveness of the system may be degraded.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for more regular updates to the CCTV system to ensure it is operating at maximum effectiveness.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-97, p. 162-165.

title: The facility does not test the CCTV system periodically. Failure to periodically test the CCTV system could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
vulnerability: The facility does not test the CCTV system periodically. Failure to periodically test the CCTV system could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
option_for_consideration: Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the CCTV system and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier, p. 126-128, p. 146-148; Patterson, D.G., 2013, Implementing Physical Protection Systems: A Practical Guide, Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 189.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
option_for_consideration: Install access control systems and/or implement access control procedures designed to permit only authorized access, detect and prevent contraband from being brought into the facility, and provide information to security personnel to facilitate the appropriate assessment and response. Effective access control can be established through the integrated use of guards, locks, credentials, and/or screening equipment (e.g., x-ray scanners, magnetometers).
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 25, p. 187.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
option_for_consideration: Establish the following access controls:

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
option_for_consideration: Post security guards at entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches. If this is not possible, integrate CCTV or other surveillance equipment to facilitate remote monitoring of access points.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 325.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
option_for_consideration: Implement screening operations for the facility. Available screening options include, but are not limited to, x-ray scanners, magnetometers, package searches, and physical searches. Post signage to notify potential entrants that screening is a prerequisite for entry into the facility.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 264.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for employees.
option_for_consideration: Issue photo ID badges to all employees. Implement a process for badge verification before employees gain access to the facility. Require badges to be displayed onsite at all times.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report - Access Control Systems, p. 22.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Review all requests for visitor access.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to screen visitor requests (e.g., with local law enforcement) to identify issues before authorizing access.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Maintain a list of regular visitors to the facility.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Limit visitor access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Require visitors to sign in and sign out.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report - Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Issue badges to visitors and require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility. Collect badges when visits are complete.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report - Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for visitors.
option_for_consideration: Escort visitors as necessary, either at all times or only in sensitive/critical areas.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
option_for_consideration: Maintain a list of regular contractors/vendors.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
option_for_consideration: Limit contractor/vendor access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
option_for_consideration: Require contractors/vendors to sign in and sign out.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
option_for_consideration: Issue badges to contractors/vendors and require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility. Collect badges when visits are complete.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 22.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for contractors/vendors.
option_for_consideration: Escort contractors/vendors as necessary, either at all times or only in sensitive/critical areas.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 22.

title: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for customers/patrons/public.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control policies/procedures for customers/patrons/public.
option_for_consideration: Limit customer/patron/public access to the facility at a level consistent with onsite safety and security requirements.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 261.

title: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
option_for_consideration: Post security guards at entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches on special event days.

title: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
option_for_consideration: Implement screening operations for the facility. Available screening options include, but are not limited to, x-ray scanners, magnetometers, bag searches, and physical searches. Post signage to notify potential entrants that screening is a prerequisite for entry into the facility.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 264.

title: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no access control procedures/policies for special events.
option_for_consideration: Resources related to access controls for special events and other security-related topics are available from the Commercial Facilities Sector-Specific Agency. Resources are publicly available through the DHS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/commercial-facilities-sector. Such resources include the Check It! video which provides information to help employees conduct bag searches properly. Additional resources are available through HSIN. Consult your local PSA to become a member of the HSIN community. Information about HSIN is also available via the DHS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/homeland-security-information-network.

title: The facility does not have a badging system.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a badging system.
option_for_consideration: Issue photo ID badges to all employees. Implement a process for badge verification before employees gain access to the facility. Collect employee badges from terminated personnel.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 22.

title: The facility does not have a badging system.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a badging system.
option_for_consideration: Issue unique badges (e.g., color-coded) to visitors, contractors, vendors, cleaning crews, and temporary employees. Collect badges when visits are complete.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 22-23.

title: The facility does not have a badging system.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a badging system.
option_for_consideration: Require badges to be displayed at all times at the facility.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 22.

title: Although the facility has a badging system in place, it is not strictly enforced.
vulnerability: Although the facility has a badging system in place, it is not strictly enforced.
option_for_consideration: Enforce badging system requirements consistently.

title: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Establish a keycard control program that includes a database of cardholders and a system for the retrieval of keycards from terminated personnel. Regularly review the keycard database and access activity reports.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 262-263.

title: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to establish access levels (i.e., to sensitive or critical areas) using the keycard system.

title: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to continuously monitor keycard access control system alarm and event activity.

title: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Explore options (i.e., physical barriers, active monitoring by posted security personnel) to establish anti-passback controls that prevent cardholders from passing their keycards to grant access to someone else outside the secured area.
source_ref: Baker, P.R., D.J. Benny, The Complete Guide to Physical Security, 2nd Edition, Boca Raton, FL: Taylor & Francis Group, p. 99.

title: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks keycard (e.g., proximity card, swipe card) control policies and procedures. Without controls, keycards may be easily misused, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Establish a "no piggybacking" policy that requires everyone to use their own keycards for access. Explore options to enforce this policy, such as physical barriers (i.e., mantraps) and active monitoring by posted security personnel.
source_ref: Bacik, S., 2008, Building an Effective Information Security Policy Architecture, Boca Raton, FL: CRC Press, p. 138.

title: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
vulnerability: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
option_for_consideration: Identify sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, mailrooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) that need access controls. Implement access controls (e.g., locks) for these areas as necessary.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 21.

title: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
vulnerability: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to restrict access to sensitive/critical areas using the existing keycard system.

title: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
vulnerability: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to restrict access to sensitive/critical areas using a keycard system.

title: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
vulnerability: Although access controls are enforced at the site perimeter and/or building entrance(s), the facility has limited or no internal access controls.
option_for_consideration: Post security guards at access points to sensitive/critical areas to enforce access control procedures such as credential checks and searches. If this is not possible, integrate CCTV or other surveillance equipment to facilitate remote monitoring of access points.

title: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
vulnerability: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
option_for_consideration: Identify areas within the facility that need additional access controls. Implement controls (e.g., locks) for these areas as necessary.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 21.

title: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
vulnerability: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to establish access levels to sensitive/critical areas using the existing keycard system.

title: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
vulnerability: Facility personnel have unrestricted access throughout the site. Controls are not in place to restrict employee access to sensitive/critical areas (e.g., control rooms, server rooms, fuel or chemical storage areas, utility service areas) to only those with a valid need for admittance.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to establish access levels to sensitive/critical areas using a keycard system.

title: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Establish a key control program that includes a system for the retrieval of keys from terminated personnel and a formal inventory of key assignments.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 141-142.

title: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that keys cannot be easily duplicated. Options include patented and restricted key profiles with controlled manufacturing.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 141.

title: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Limit the use of master keys for secured sensitive or critical areas to select security personnel.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 141.

title: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
vulnerability: The facility lacks key control policies and procedures. Without controls, keys may be easily misused or duplicated without authorization, and access control to the facility may be compromised.
option_for_consideration: Designate a key control officer to manage the key control program and conduct regular key inventories.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 141-142.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Post security guards at vehicle entrances to facilitate access control procedures such as credential checks and searches. If this is not possible, integrate CCTV or other surveillance equipment to facilitate remote monitoring of vehicle access points.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 325.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: In the absence of a security force, pursue a policy in which facility employees are empowered to report any suspicious vehicles or activity in parking lots or structures.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of executing searches on all vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Execute random searches on vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility. Evaluate the need to conduct searches on all vehicles at elevated threat levels.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Positively identify all vehicles and drivers that enter the facility. Deny access to suspicious vehicles/drivers and those with improper documentation (e.g., cargo manifest). Deny access to drivers who fail to provide identification and/or submit to inspection.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Maintain a database of employee vehicles. Issue permits for designated areas.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
vulnerability: The facility has limited or no vehicle access control policies/procedures.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the feasibility of using centralized parking and shuttle services to keep vehicles away from critical areas.
source_ref: DHS, 2011, Industry Protective Measures Report – Access Control Systems, p. 23.

title: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
vulnerability: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
option_for_consideration: Consult your local PSA for information about the "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. For more information, visit the DHS Website at http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something%E2%84%A2-campaign.

title: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
vulnerability: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
option_for_consideration: Access video and audio public service announcements encouraging the public to contact local authorities if they see suspicious activity at http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something-public-service-announcements.

title: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
vulnerability: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
option_for_consideration: DHS is working to expand the "If You See Something, Say Something™" campaign throughout the country by partnering with a variety of entities including transportation systems, universities, States, cities, sports leagues, and local law enforcement. If you are interested in getting your group involved, please contact 202-282-8010.
source_ref: DHS, undated, "If You See Something, Say Something™,” http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something%E2%84%A2-campaign, accessed June 4, 2014.

title: Facility personnel are aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign, but do not use materials/messaging to provide direction for detecting and reporting suspicious activity.
vulnerability: Facility personnel are aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign, but do not use materials/messaging to provide direction for detecting and reporting suspicious activity.
option_for_consideration: Consult your local PSA for information about available "If You See Something, Say Something™" materials/messaging.

title: Facility personnel are not aware of the PS-Prep™ certification program. PS-Prep™ is a voluntary private sector preparedness accreditation and certification program designed to improve the preparedness of private sector and not-profit organizations through conformance to consensus-based preparedness standards and best practices.
vulnerability: Facility personnel are not aware of the PS-Prep™ certification program. PS-Prep™ is a voluntary private sector preparedness accreditation and certification program designed to improve the preparedness of private sector and not-profit organizations through conformance to consensus-based preparedness standards and best practices.
option_for_consideration: Consult your local PSA for information about PS-Prep™. Information is also available through the FEMA Website, at http://www.fema.gov/about-ps-preptm.

title: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
vulnerability: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
option_for_consideration: Research, review, and evaluate established risk management standards. Select the approach that is most relevant to the facility's particular circumstances.
source_ref: Biringer, B.E., R.V. Matalucci, and S.L. O’Connor, 2007, Security Risk Assessment and Management – A Professional Practice Guide for Protecting Buildings and Infrastructures, Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, p. 175.

title: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
vulnerability: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
option_for_consideration: Engage the risk manager/risk management department in the research, review, and evaluation of established risk management standards. Select the approach that is most relevant to the facility's particular circumstances.
source_ref: Biringer, B.E., R.V. Matalucci, and S.L. O’Connor, 2007, Security Risk Assessment and Management – A Professional Practice Guide for Protecting Buildings and Infrastructures, Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, p. 175.

title: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
vulnerability: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
option_for_consideration: Consult your local PSA for information about established risk management standards.

title: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
vulnerability: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
option_for_consideration: In May 2014, DHS announced the adoption of the following updated preparedness standards: ASIS International SPC.1-2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use Standards, ISO 22301:2012: Societal security -- Business continuity management systems, and NFPA 1600: Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs, 2013 edition. More information about these standards is available through the FEMA Website, at http://www.fema.gov/about-ps-preptm.

title: The facility does not have a written MOU/MOA with the primary law enforcement agency. Formal agreements with first responders expedite assistance and the provision of special services as necessary.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written MOU/MOA with the primary law enforcement agency. Formal agreements with first responders expedite assistance and the provision of special services as necessary.
option_for_consideration: Establish an MOU/MOA with the agency to define the assistance and special services the agency will provide in the event of a threat, attack, or incident. An MOU/MOA can address items such as points of contact, notification procedures, exchange of threat information, access to the facility, hazards present on the facility, and participation in regular consults, drills, and exercises.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

title: The facility does not have a written MOU/MOA with the primary law enforcement agency. Formal agreements with first responders expedite assistance and the provision of special services as necessary.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written MOU/MOA with the primary law enforcement agency. Formal agreements with first responders expedite assistance and the provision of special services as necessary.
option_for_consideration: Establish a liaison with the agency. Explore the option of creating a formal agreement for assistance and special services.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

title: The primary law enforcement agency has not conducted an onsite visit of the facility. Onsite visits can familiarize first responders with the facility, key personnel, site layout, and other issues that would enhance incident response.
vulnerability: The primary law enforcement agency has not conducted an onsite visit of the facility. Onsite visits can familiarize first responders with the facility, key personnel, site layout, and other issues that would enhance incident response.
option_for_consideration: Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

title: The primary law enforcement agency has not conducted an onsite visit of the facility. Onsite visits can familiarize first responders with the facility, key personnel, site layout, and other issues that would enhance incident response.
vulnerability: The primary law enforcement agency has not conducted an onsite visit of the facility. Onsite visits can familiarize first responders with the facility, key personnel, site layout, and other issues that would enhance incident response.
option_for_consideration: Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

title: The facility does not have interoperable communications with the primary law enforcement agency. Without interoperable communications, the facility cannot communicate with first responders in order to coordinate security and response activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have interoperable communications with the primary law enforcement agency. Without interoperable communications, the facility cannot communicate with first responders in order to coordinate security and response activities.
option_for_consideration: Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

title: The facility does not have interoperable communications with the primary law enforcement agency. Without interoperable communications, the facility cannot communicate with first responders in order to coordinate security and response activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have interoperable communications with the primary law enforcement agency. Without interoperable communications, the facility cannot communicate with first responders in order to coordinate security and response activities.
option_for_consideration: Explore the option of an interconnect system, such as a gateway that can allow communication between radio systems that are otherwise incompatible because they operate on different frequency bands or using different technologies.
source_ref: FCC, 2008, FCC Report to Congress: Vulnerability Assessment and Feasibility of Creating a Back-Up Emergency Communications System, http://transition.fcc.gov/pshs/docs/clearinghouse/case-studies/ECS-vulnerability-assessment-report.pdf, accessed June 25, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance, when designing an appropriate lighting situation, because of their effect on project sustainability.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-67 to 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 136-138.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of implementing the following:

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Installing two or more lighting units at pedestrian entrances, that provide adequate illumination for recognizing individuals and examining credentials.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Installing two lighting units at vehicle access points, to facilitate the complete inspection of passenger cars, trucks, etc., as well as their contents and passengers.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Providing a minimum surface lighting average of 4 horizontal foot-candles at gates to help ensure adequate lighting for pedestrians, islands, and guards in approach and exit zones. However, this level is inadequate in entry control zones where IDs will be checked. Guardhouse and ID check positions should have a minimum of 10 foot-candles, with 20 foot-candles preferred. (A foot-candle is a unit of illuminance on a surface that is everywhere 1 foot from a uniform point source of light of one candle and equal to 1 lumen per square foot. To convert foot-candles to watts, first convert foot-candles into lumens, then convert lumens into watts. One foot-candle equals 10.76 lumens, and 1 lumen equals 0.001496 watts.)
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-68, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Merriam Webster, 2014, "Foot-candle," Dictionary, http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/foot-candle, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Using glare projection for isolated fenced perimeters and keeping security patrol routes unlit. (Isolated fenced perimeters are fence lines around areas where the fence is 100 feet or more from buildings or operating areas. The approach area is clear of obstruction for 100 or more feet outside of the fence. Other personnel do not use the area.)
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Using controlled lighting and keeping security patrol routes in relative darkness for semi-isolated fenced perimeters. (Semi-isolated, fenced perimeters are fence lines where approach areas are clear of obstruction for 60 to 100 feet outside of the fence. The general public or facility personnel seldom have reason to be in the area.)
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Using controlled lighting for non-isolated fenced perimeters. It may not be practical to keep the patrol area dark. (Non-isolated fenced perimeters are fence lines immediately adjacent to operating areas. Outsiders or facility personnel may move about freely in this approach area.)
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-12, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that lighting is compatible with CCTV systems, as the cameras may need high intensity, low intensity, or infrared light for proper operation.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-68, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information about security lighting.

title: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas does not overlap. Lighting that does not overlap can cause shadows or dark areas where intruders can go undetected.
vulnerability: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas does not overlap. Lighting that does not overlap can cause shadows or dark areas where intruders can go undetected.
option_for_consideration: Update the lighting system to include overlapping cones of light to eliminate or reduce shadows and dark areas.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-68, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas is uneven and dissimilar, causing glare and shadows, and resulting in inconsistent coverage, which can produce dark areas and shadows where intruders can go undetected.
