vulnerability: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas is uneven and dissimilar, causing glare and shadows, and resulting in inconsistent coverage, which can produce dark areas and shadows where intruders can go undetected.
option_for_consideration: Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-5, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas is uneven and dissimilar, causing glare and shadows, and resulting in inconsistent coverage, which can produce dark areas and shadows where intruders can go undetected.
vulnerability: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas is uneven and dissimilar, causing glare and shadows, and resulting in inconsistent coverage, which can produce dark areas and shadows where intruders can go undetected.
option_for_consideration: Update the lighting system to ensure illumination uniformity, so that security personnel can see ahead and to the sides with an absence of dark areas caused by shadows. Lighting should be brightest in secure areas, with the light gradually less in areas adjacent to high-illumination areas.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-5, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas have lights that are in need of repair or maintenance. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas have lights that are in need of repair or maintenance. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Establish a maintenance protocol to ensure that system components are regularly inspected and repaired or replaced as necessary. Ensure that lighting fixtures are clean and properly aimed.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014; Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 185-186.

title: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas have burned out bulbs in critical locations. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Fences, gates, and/or parking areas have burned out bulbs in critical locations. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Establish a maintenance protocol to ensure that system components are regularly inspected and repaired or replaced as necessary. Ensure that lighting fixtures are clean and properly aimed. Replace working bulbs on a schedule based on their life expectancy.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014; Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 185-186.

title: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas does not have backup power. In the event of a power outage, these areas would not have emergency lighting to continue to provide illumination.
vulnerability: Lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas does not have backup power. In the event of a power outage, these areas would not have emergency lighting to continue to provide illumination.
option_for_consideration: Expand the coverage of the backup power supply to include emergency lighting for these areas, so that in the event of a power failure or other emergency, illumination is available.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The backup power supply covers emergency lighting for only critical locations along fences and/or gates and/or in parking areas. The backup power supply does not cover most of the existing lights.
vulnerability: The backup power supply covers emergency lighting for only critical locations along fences and/or gates and/or in parking areas. The backup power supply does not cover most of the existing lights.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that the backup power supply covers most existing lights, not only lights for critical locations.

title: Although the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas, it does not cover emergency lighting for most critical locations in these areas.
vulnerability: Although the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas, it does not cover emergency lighting for most critical locations in these areas.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that the backup power supply covers emergency lighting for most, if not all, critical locations.

title: The facility does not have portable lighting capabilities onsite to provide lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas during emergencies or elevated threat situations.
vulnerability: The facility does not have portable lighting capabilities onsite to provide lighting for fences, gates, and/or parking areas during emergencies or elevated threat situations.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of procuring portable lighting capabilities to provide coverage for these areas, as needed.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: Building entrances and delivery areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Building entrances and delivery areas are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install lighting for building entrances and delivery areas. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance because of their effect on project sustainability.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-67 to 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier, p. 136-138.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install lighting for waterside facilities. Before installation, determine the appropriate type of lighting based on the overall requirements of the site and the building (for example, continuous or standby). In addition, consider operational costs, such as life-cycle costs for energy and maintenance, when designing an appropriate lighting situation, because of their effect on project sustainability.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.4.3, p. 2-67 to 2-69, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier, p. 136-138.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Illuminating water approaches and the pier area. The lighting must not in any way violate marine rules and regulations (for example, it must not be glaring to pilots).
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Illuminating decks on open piers to at least 1 foot-candle, and the water approaches (extending to a distance of 100 feet from the pier) to at least 0.5 foot-candles. (A foot-candle is a unit of illuminance on a surface that is everywhere 1 foot from a uniform point source of light of one candle and equal to 1 lumen per square foot. To convert foot-candles to watts, first convert foot-candles into lumens, then convert lumens into watts. One foot-candle equals 10.76 lumens, and 1 lumen equals 0.001496 watts.)
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014; Merriam Webster, 2014, "Foot-candle," Dictionary, http://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/foot-candle, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Illuminating the area beneath the pier floor with small-wattage floodlights arranged on the piling.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 5-13, August, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 13, 2014.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
vulnerability: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Providing portable lighting as part of the protective lighting system for piers and docks.

title: Waterside facilities are not illuminated. A lack of quality lighting can reduce observation capabilities and diminish the safety and security posture of the facility.

vulnerability: None of the facility's employees have U.S. government security clearance. Without clearances, facility personnel cannot review classified material related to pertinent threats.
option_for_consideration: Consult your local PSA for more information about the DHS Private Sector Clearance Program, which sponsors security clearances for certain private-sector officials, including critical infrastructure owners/operators, sector leadership, and subject matter experts.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to hurricane-related flooding.
source_ref: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. If possible, move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from severe weather events such as wind. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events.
source_ref: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
option_for_consideration: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/hurricanes, for more information.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Establish specific plans and procedures for the duration of hurricane season. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available hurricane planning resources.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Establish a hurricane planning checklist that can be provided to all facility occupants ahead of hurricane season.
source_ref: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Develop a plan for dealing with power outages and loss of elevator service in high-rise buildings. Ensure the plan includes the location of alternate exits for occupants.
source_ref: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
option_for_consideration: If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.

title: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.
vulnerability: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.
option_for_consideration: If the facility does not have permanent storm shutters, establish plans to board windows with exterior-grade or marine plywood. Ensure temporary covers are readily accessible, and use a numbering or lettering system that shows which cover goes with which window.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.
vulnerability: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.
option_for_consideration: Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
option_for_consideration: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to severe flooding.
source_ref: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
option_for_consideration: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
option_for_consideration: (Ref: OFC #23) Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods, for more information.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for flooding.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for flooding.
option_for_consideration: Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from flooding damage. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available flood planning resources.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for flooding.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for flooding.
option_for_consideration: Establish a flood response checklist that can be provided to employees ahead of a flood watch and/or warning.
source_ref: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 9.

title: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of flooding.
vulnerability: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of flooding.
option_for_consideration: (Ref: OFC #20) Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of modifying or retrofitting the facility to make it safer during earthquakes and more resistant to earthquake damage and disruption. The optimal combination earthquake risk-reduction measures for a facility will depend on factors such as the potential severity of the earthquake hazards in the area, the current condition of the facility, whether the facility is owned or leased, and how vulnerable facility operations are to facility damage and associated downtime. Consult QuakeSmart, at http://www.fema.gov/quakesmart, for information about how to assess facility risks and how to develop and implement a plan to cost-effectively mitigate those risks.
source_ref: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: If the facility has cripple walls, brace the walls by adding 2-inch by 4-inch boards between vertical studs at the top and bottom of the walls and, if necessary, at other locations between the studs. Consult the FEMA flyer Brace Cripple Walls, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2008, Brace Cripple Walls, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: Anchor any sill plates. Sill plates rest directly on top of a foundation, and bolting the plates directly into the foundation provides extra stability. Consult the FEMA flyer Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2008, Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting existing gas or water lines with flexible connections to prevent damage. Consult the FEMA flyer Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2008, Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of anchoring heavy equipment directly to the floor or another suitable part of the building. If this is not possible, mount equipment on vibration isolators. Consult the FEMA flyer Anchor Equipment Properly, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

title: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
option_for_consideration: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/earthquakes, for more information.

title: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for earthquakes.
vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for earthquakes.
option_for_consideration: Establish earthquake emergency plans and procedures. Emphasize safe places for building occupants to "drop, cover, and hold on" during earthquake shaking. Establish safe locations where people can rendezvous when the shaking has stopped and it is safe and advisable to evacuate the facility. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available earthquake planning resources.

vulnerability: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.

source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.
vulnerability: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.
option_for_consideration: Allow only permit parking by authorized individuals of inspected vehicles within 400 feet of the facility.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.
vulnerability: Vehicles can park within 400 feet of the facility. Access to the parking area is uncontrolled. The lack of rigorous vehicle access control procedures increases the vulnerability of the facility to a vehicle-borne attack.
option_for_consideration: (1) Determine the facility’s susceptibility to blast. Consult Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information, especially Chapters 2 and 3: Chapter 2 discusses blast design concerns and protective measures for areas surrounding buildings. Chapter 3 discusses the nature of explosive blasts, their effects on buildings and occupants, and the concept of levels of protection. In addition, review the DHS Bomb Threat Stand-off Chart, available through the Lessons Learned Information Sharing national, online network, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/DHS-BombThreatChart-6-5-09.pdf. (2) Based on the facility’s potential vulnerabilities, explore the feasibility of conducting a preliminary blast analysis using simple, commercially available building modeling and software designed to support the ability to address blast mitigation and potential VBIED threats, such as A.T.-BLAST by ARA Applied Research Associates. (3) Based on the results from the preliminary blast analysis, explore the feasibility of consulting with an engineering firm that specializes in structural fragility to procure a professional blast assessment to inform capital investments intended to protect personnel and the facility against blast.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing high curbs and other measures to keep vehicles from departing the roadway in an effort to avoid security countermeasures.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.2.1, p. 6-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of restricting or eliminating parking along the curb, and/or eliminating street-side loading zones for high-risk assets.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
option_for_consideration: Request appropriate permits to restrict parking in curb lanes in densely populated areas to company-owned vehicles or key employee vehicles.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.7.2, p. 6-26, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available adjacent to the facility, on the street.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of prohibiting street parking or enacting lane closure as a temporary measure during times of increased alert. Carefully plan such closures against elevated threats to avoid improvising with unaesthetic and disruptive measures.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.2.1, p. 6-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Parking within the building leaves the facility highly susceptible to accidental or intentional damage, but if parking elsewhere is not an option, apply the following restrictions:
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Permit parking only for company vehicles and employees of the building.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Require proper credentials for all passengers.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Conduct full vehicle inspections.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Require ID checks for visitor parking.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-24, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available under the building.
option_for_consideration: Ensure under-building parking is well-lighted and free of places of concealment and dead-end parking spaces.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 6.7.3, p. 6-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Uncontrolled parking is available above the building.
vulnerability: Uncontrolled parking is available above the building.
option_for_consideration: Maximize visibility for surveillance into, out of, and across the parking garage.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of providing 24/7 CCTV monitoring for the parking area. Although CCTV coverage may need to be limited in its scope, it can still provide satisfactory coverage.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 166.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Install cameras in areas such as the main entrance, at all vehicle access control points, guard booths, cashier booths, and elevator lobbies on every floor. This will ensure more than adequate security.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 141-142.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Position the cameras to view the parking ramps and driveways as well as the intercom stations.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Ensure lighting adequately illuminates activity in the parking area for viewing and videotaping.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.8, p. 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force. Routes and times for patrols should also be varied at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-2 to A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of using facility employees to monitor uncontrolled parking areas.

title: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
vulnerability: Parking in the uncontrolled parking area is not monitored in any way.
option_for_consideration: Provide suspicious activity awareness training to facility employees, including topics related to uncontrolled parking areas. Provide options (e.g., hotline, direct phone or radio communications with security operations) for employees to report suspicious activity, potential threats, and incidents.

title: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
vulnerability: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
option_for_consideration: Implement SOPs to address unauthorized, suspicious, and extended-stay vehicles. Processes to consider include reporting vehicles to security and/or local law enforcement, and contracting with an area towing company for vehicle removal.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 3.3, p. 3-10 to 3-14, section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
vulnerability: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
option_for_consideration: Train security personnel on the SOPs to address unauthorized, suspicious, and extended-stay vehicles.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 3.3, p. 3-10 to 3-14, section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
vulnerability: There are no procedures or policies to identify and act on unauthorized vehicles parked in the uncontrolled parking area for an extended period.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that the parking area is covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force to ensure early identification of unauthorized parked vehicles.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-2 to A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Explore options to staff the controlled parking area. If possible, employ security personnel to screen drivers and/or inspect vehicles.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-11 to 5-14, section 5.8, p. 5-23 to 5-25, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: If SOPs for vehicle inspections are implemented, ensure that inspection areas are large enough to accommodate a minimum of one vehicle and a pull-out lane. They should also be covered and capable of accommodating the inspection of the undercarriage plus overhead inspection equipment. Inspection bays that can be closed to protect inspection equipment and staff in the event of bad weather are ideal.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-12, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, the controlled parking area is unattended. No personnel are involved in providing vehicle access control for the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Investigate available inspection technologies (e.g., above-vehicle and under-vehicle surveillance systems, ion scanning, and x-ray equipment) when sizing and designing vehicle inspection areas.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.5.3, p. 5-12, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, the controlled parking area is attended by a ticket or toll agent. Although the attendant may be able to report unauthorized vehicle access or suspicious activity, security personnel staffing this control point could provide a more rapid response.
vulnerability: During business hours, the controlled parking area is attended by a ticket or toll agent. Although the attendant may be able to report unauthorized vehicle access or suspicious activity, security personnel staffing this control point could provide a more rapid response.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the need to provide security staffing for vehicle access control points. Security force personnel may be trained and authorized to conduct vehicle searches and deny vehicle access.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, vehicle searches are not conducted as vehicles enter the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, vehicle searches are not conducted as vehicles enter the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of executing searches on all vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, vehicle searches are not conducted as vehicles enter the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, vehicle searches are not conducted as vehicles enter the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Execute random searches on vehicles entering the controlled parking area for the facility. Evaluate the need to conduct searches on all vehicles at elevated threat levels.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 2.5, p. 2-30, section 5.5, p. 5-8 to 5-15, section 6.5, p. 6-22 to 6-23, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During business hours, locks or technology controls are not used to control access to the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During business hours, locks or technology controls are not used to control access to the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Employ a locking mechanism to help ensure that the controlled parking area remains secured during business hours.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: During off-business hours, locks or technology controls are not used to control access to the controlled parking area.
vulnerability: During off-business hours, locks or technology controls are not used to control access to the controlled parking area.
option_for_consideration: Employ a locking mechanism to help ensure that the controlled parking area remains secured during off-business hours.
source_ref: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-3, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install fencing appropriate for the facility type. Determine the appropriate role of the fencing either to demarcate the boundary of the site to protect against trespassing; provide access control by channeling individuals through authorized access points; and/or to protect against unauthorized entry by providing increased access delay and more time for assessment.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration:
Consider the installation of fencing with the following attributes:
Type
- chain link OR
- anti-climb aluminum or steel chain link OR
- standard aluminum or steel chain link OR
- concrete OR
- brick and mortar OR
- steel OR
- wrought iron
Height
- 7 ft. high OR
- 8 ft. high OR
- 9 ft. high
Base of Fence
- anchored base
Outriggers
- outriggers OR
- outriggers angled at 45 degrees OR
- outriggers in a "Y"/"V" configuration
Enhancements
- K-rated for vehicle penetration 
- cabling to provide additional crash resistance
- coiled razor wire along the top of the fence
- coiled barbed wire along the top of the fence
- coiled razor wire along the bottom of the fence 
- coiled barbed wire along the bottom of the fence 
- spikes along the top to prevent scaling 
- privacy screening

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the option of providing enhanced intrusion detection with countermeasures such as surveillance, alarms, or patrols.
source_ref: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 91-113.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Design the fence line to maximize natural surveillance from the street to the facility and from the facility to the street, and minimize opportunities for intruders to hide.
source_ref: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install fencing appropriate for the facility type. Determine the appropriate role of the fencing either to demarcate the boundary of the site to protect against trespassing; provide access control by channeling individuals through authorized access points
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-12, p. 26-28, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of providing penetration delay against vehicles by adding cable system fences. Cable fencing may be used in conjunction with fences and gates.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-11, p. 24-25, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014; FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-54, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at a http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration: Consult UFC 4-022-03, Security Fences and Gates, available at http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, for more information.

title: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
option_for_consideration:
Employ Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) principles and/or barriers (e.g., bollards, decorative flower pots, high curbs, shallow ditches) to provide enhanced penetration delay.
Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

title: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install additional fencing to enclose the facility to the maximum extent possible.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of improving the landscape to supplement the existing facility perimeter fence. Landscaping examples include earthen berms, low-growing shrubs, plants, or trees. Shrubs or plants with spines or thorns may be used in conjunction with the fence to provide increased penetration delay.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.6, p. 2-73 to 2-74, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 60.

title: Although 100% of the facility is enclosed, 100% of SAAs are not enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
vulnerability: Although 100% of the facility is enclosed, 100% of SAAs are not enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install fencing to fully enclose SAAs, where feasible.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed, and less than 100% of SAAs are enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
vulnerability: Less than 100% of the facility is enclosed, and less than 100% of SAAs are enclosed. The lack of fencing may allow unrestricted access to critical areas of the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install additional fencing to fully enclose the facility and SAAs, where feasible.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: A standard aluminum or steel chain-link fence may be easily traversed and provides little penetration delay against determined intruders.
vulnerability: A standard aluminum or steel chain-link fence may be easily traversed and provides little penetration delay against determined intruders.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of upgrading to stronger fence construction. Options  include anti-climb aluminum or steel chain link fencing consisting of 6- to 8-gauge welded wire fabric with a center spacing of 1/2 inch or less.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 207.

title: A standard aluminum or steel chain-link fence may be easily traversed and provides little penetration delay against determined intruders.
vulnerability: A standard aluminum or steel chain-link fence may be easily traversed and provides little penetration delay against determined intruders.
option_for_consideration: Explore the option of supplementing or enhancing penetration delay with countermeasures such as surveillance, alarms, or patrols, if upgrading to a stronger fence construction is not feasible.
source_ref: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 91-113.

title: A wood fence may be only for demarcating a boundary or for decoration, but not for security purposes. A wood fence may be easily traversed or penetrated by determined intruders.
vulnerability: A wood fence may be only for demarcating a boundary or for decoration, but not for security purposes. A wood fence may be easily traversed or penetrated by determined intruders.
option_for_consideration: Replace the existing fence with one providing more penetration delay appropriate for the facility type.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

title: Fences less than 7 feet tall do little more than demarcate a boundary.
vulnerability: Fences less than 7 feet tall do little more than demarcate a boundary.
option_for_consideration: Increase the height of the fence or install outriggers with barbed wire to enhance the height and penetration delay provided by the fence, if more than boundary demarcation is appropriate. The fence should be at least 7-9 feet high. The higher the fence, the more difficult and time-consuming it is to climb.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 96.

title: A fence between 5-6 feet in height is not suitable for security purposes. A security fence should be no less than 7 feet (excluding enhancements). A shorter fence can be easily traversed by intruders.
vulnerability: A fence between 5-6 feet in height is not suitable for security purposes. A security fence should be no less than 7 feet (excluding enhancements). A shorter fence can be easily traversed by intruders.
option_for_consideration: Replace the existing fence with higher fencing appropriate for the facility type.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., ch. 9, p. 206.

title: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
vulnerability: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
option_for_consideration: Anchor the base of the fence to protect against access by crawling under or raising lower portion of fence. The fence should also be installed to preclude access made possible by ground erosion.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275.

title: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
vulnerability: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
option_for_consideration: Extend the fencing fabric to within 2 inches of firm ground, and anchor the base of the fence using horizontal bottom rails, tension wires, concrete curbs, sills, sheet piling, piping, or other inexpensive materials. Horizontal bottom rails, concrete curbs, or sills can mitigate the ability to lift the fence fabric by hand (e.g., no more than 5 inches).
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
vulnerability: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
option_for_consideration: Bury the fence fabric (e.g., 12 inches into the ground) for additional security. This may also prevent the fencing fabric from being lifted (for example, by more than 5 inches). However, corrosion of the buried fabric must be monitored.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: Although the fence is equipped with outriggers, they are angled toward the facility and do not effectively deter access over the top of the fence from outside the perimeter.
vulnerability: Although the fence is equipped with outriggers, they are angled toward the facility and do not effectively deter access over the top of the fence from outside the perimeter.
option_for_consideration: Equip the fence with outriggers that are angled out and away from the facility to make climbing the fence more difficult and to prevent ladders from being placed against it. The overhang should extend far enough to increase the height of the fence by at least 1 foot. Consult UFC 4-022-03, Security Fences and Gates, available at http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, and Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria, available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp, for more information.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 274-275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.
vulnerability: The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.
option_for_consideration: Install outriggers with connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals, along the top of the fence. Outriggers typically face outward (i.e., away from the facility) and upward at approximately a 45-degree angle.
source_ref: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29 to 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.
vulnerability: The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.
option_for_consideration: Install outriggers (support arms) at 45-degree angles in a single arm (towards the threat side) or “Y”/”V” configuration, constructed of a single or double outrigger consisting of 18-inch (457 mm) arm(s), each having three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals along the top of the fence. The outriggers should provide a minimum of an additional 12 inches to the fence height. Consult Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria, available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp, for more information.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-9.2, p. 22-24, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: The fence is topped with outriggers (support arms), but connecting wire (e.g., barbed or razor wire) is not installed on the supporting arms. Without connecting wire, the outriggers serve only as extensions of the fence posts.
vulnerability: The fence is topped with outriggers (support arms), but connecting wire (e.g., barbed or razor wire) is not installed on the supporting arms. Without connecting wire, the outriggers serve only as extensions of the fence posts.
option_for_consideration: Install connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire, at regular intervals along the top of the fence.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-9.2, p. 22-24, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: The fence lacks privacy screening to obscure the observation of onsite facilities, critical  equipment, etc.
vulnerability: The fence lacks privacy screening to obscure the observation of onsite facilities, critical  equipment, etc.
option_for_consideration: Add slats or mesh fabric to the fence to provide screening, if privacy is appropriate. Such screening can provide opportunities for cover and concealment and preclude visual assessment by security force. However, these techniques should not be used if air flow through the fence is important.
source_ref: McDonald, J.D., 2012, Electric Power Substations Engineering, 3rd Edition, Boca Raton, FL: CRC Press, p. 16-13.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Install enhancements to the fence to improve its effectiveness and penetration delay.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Eliminate the top rail of a fence to make it more difficult to grasp.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Integrate other security systems with the fence, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, as appropriate.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of integrating chain-link fabric into perimeter fencing which covers the chain-link fencing to prevent penetration and provide concealment.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 270-271.
