title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Apply welded wire-mesh fabric to the fence. Welded wire-mesh fabric is welded tightly together to prevent toe or finger hold and adds greater resistance against cutting.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of adding barbed, taped concertina to the top of the fence. Taped concertina can significantly decrease the ability to scale a fence.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of installing "tangle-foot wire." Tangle-foot wire is barbed wire strung across two posts no higher than 12 inches. It is used outside a perimeter fence or between double fences to provide extra protection from an intruder. Liability issues should be considered when installing tangle-foot wire.
source_ref: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of adding tunneling preventive measures for gaps that would allow for entry under the fence.
source_ref: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-12.8, p. 34-37, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

title: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
vulnerability: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
option_for_consideration: Establish clear zones on both sides of the fence to provide an unobstructed view to enhance detection and assessment. The dimensions of clears zones may vary depending on the asset being protected and level of protection required. Provide as much open space as possible between the fence and the facility.
source_ref: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

title: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
vulnerability: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
option_for_consideration: Ensure clear zones are free of debris, vegetation, and any items that could provide cover and concealment. When it is impossible to have adequate clear zones because of property lines or natural or manmade features, it may be necessary to increase the height of the perimeter barrier, increase security-patrol coverage, and/or add more security lighting along that portion of the perimeter. Failure to establish and maintain a clear zone along the fence minimizes its effectiveness. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 272; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39, p. 91-113, p. 259.

title: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
vulnerability: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that for high security applications a clear zone of 20 feet or more separates a perimeter barrier from exterior structures, parking areas, and natural features. A clear zone of 50 feet or more should separate a perimeter barrier from structures within the protected area, except when a building wall constitutes part of a perimeter barrier.Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: Muuss, J.P., and D. Rabern, 2006, The Complete Guide for CPP Examination Preparation, Boca Raton, FL: Auerbach Publications, p. 174.

title: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
vulnerability: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
option_for_consideration: Implement a grounds maintenance program to check and clear vegetation regularly from the fence's clear zone. Vegetation should not exceed 8 inches in height. Also, because fallen trees can damage fences, management of tall trees in the vicinity of the fence is important, especially in areas where a fence passes through a remote area.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

title: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.
vulnerability: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.
option_for_consideration: Remove all objects near the fence that would aid traversing it. Keep a clear zone free of any item that could aid an intruder in breeching or circumventing the perimeter fence.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 272.

title: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.
vulnerability: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.
option_for_consideration: Ensure security force roving patrols include surveillance of the perimeter fence and objects/structures that remain near the fence line.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

title: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
vulnerability: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
option_for_consideration: Install warning signs that are easy to understand along the fence. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages. Post signage at intervals of no more than 100 feet.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
vulnerability: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
option_for_consideration: Post visible, well-placed warning signs on the fence. Signs may act as a deterrent and/or provide safety information for unauthorized personnel. Signs are usually placed on boundary fences, typically at 50-foot intervals, to indicate ownership and to warn of possible danger within. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install gates, providing appropriate levels of access control and/or penetration delay against individuals, at identified gaps in perimeter fencing. Install barriers/systems at gates to provide vehicle access control and/or vehicle penetration delay, as appropriate.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 112-113, p. 270; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 47, p. 180, p. 263-265.

title: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install vehicle gates for restricted areas so that they are set back from the roadway to ensure that temporary delays caused by identification checks will not cause traffic hazards. Provide sufficient space to allow for spot checks, inspections, and searches of vehicles without impeding the flow of traffic.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 112-113, p. 270; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 264-265.

title: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Integrate other security systems, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, at gates, as appropriate.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.

title: The facility has a manually operated drop-arm gate that provides little to no protection against vehicle penetration.
vulnerability: The facility has a manually operated drop-arm gate that provides little to no protection against vehicle penetration.
option_for_consideration: Replace drop-arm gates with more robust gates that provide appropriate levels of access control and/or penetration delay.

title: Moveable bollards may be an option to slow traffic, but they would be unable to completely stop a vehicle traveling at a high rate of speed unless they were hydraulically operated.
vulnerability: Moveable bollards may be an option to slow traffic, but they would be unable to completely stop a vehicle traveling at a high rate of speed unless they were hydraulically operated.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of establishing non-moveable bollards or other non-moveable barriers. Stationary bollards or barriers can significantly decrease the chance of a vehicle penetrating a facility.
source_ref: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 212.

title: The vehicle gate is not topped with outriggers, which makes the gate more scalable.
vulnerability: The vehicle gate is not topped with outriggers, which makes the gate more scalable.
option_for_consideration: Install outriggers, where appropriate, to increase the overall height of the gate protect against traversing.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 274-275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: Although the vehicle gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter and delay penetration (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming).
vulnerability: Although the vehicle gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter and delay penetration (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming).
option_for_consideration: Install enhancements for the gate to improve its effectiveness to deter and delay penetration.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: The vehicle gate is less than 7 feet tall. Gates less than 7 feet tall may be easily traversed.
vulnerability: The vehicle gate is less than 7 feet tall. Gates less than 7 feet tall may be easily traversed.
option_for_consideration: Increase the height of the gate or install outriggers with barbed wire to enhance the height and security of the gate. The gate should be at least 7 to 9 feet high. The higher the gate, the more difficult and time-consuming it is to climb. Consult UFC 4-022-03, Security Fences and Gates, available at http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 96.

title: The vehicle gate is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
vulnerability: The vehicle gate is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
option_for_consideration: Post visible, well-placed warning signs on the gate. Signs may act as a deterrent and/or provide safety information for unauthorized personnel. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages.
source_ref: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: There are no gates at pedestrian openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals with unrestricted access to the facility.
vulnerability: There are no gates at pedestrian openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals with unrestricted access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Add appropriate gates with entry control measures to prevent unrestricted access pedestrian to the facility (e.g., full height turnstile, swing gate).
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: Although the pedestrian gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized access to the facility.
vulnerability: Although the pedestrian gate provides a clear boundary marker and alerts individuals to restricted areas, the gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized access to the facility.
option_for_consideration: Install enhancements for the gate to improve its effectiveness to deter unauthorized access to the facility.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: There are no gates at rail access points to the rail yard and yard facilities. The lack of such gates may allow unrestricted access to the rail yard and yard facilities.
vulnerability: There are no gates at rail access points to the rail yard and yard facilities. The lack of such gates may allow unrestricted access to the rail yard and yard facilities.
option_for_consideration: Add appropriate gates to prevent open access to rail yard and yard facilities.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; U.S. Government Accountability Office, 2009, Freight Rail Security: Actions Have Been Taken to Enhance Security, but the Federal Strategy Can Be Strengthened and Security Efforts Better Monitored (GAO-09-243), April, http://www.gao.gov/new.items/d09243.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: The rail gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized rail access to the facility. Additional enhancements can prevent defeat of the gate.
vulnerability: The rail gate lacks enhancements that would increase its effectiveness to deter unauthorized rail access to the facility. Additional enhancements can prevent defeat of the gate.
option_for_consideration: Install enhancements for the gate to improve its effectiveness and durability. Enhancements that may be applied to existing gates include train derailers.
source_ref: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; U.S. Government Accountability Office, 2009, Freight Rail Security: Actions Have Been Taken to Enhance Security, but the Federal Strategy Can Be Strengthened and Security Efforts Better Monitored (GAO-09-243), April, http://www.gao.gov/new.items/d09243.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

title: The facility does not have a designated business continuity manager. The lack of a designated business continuity manager may hinder the implementation of business continuity policies, programs, or procedures.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a designated business continuity manager. The lack of a designated business continuity manager may hinder the implementation of business continuity policies, programs, or procedures.
option_for_consideration: Appoint or nominate a person with appropriate seniority and authority to be accountable for business continuity management policy and implementation. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/program-management.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.2, p. 13.

title: The facility does not participate in emergency preparedness working groups.
vulnerability: The facility does not participate in emergency preparedness working groups.
option_for_consideration: Join relevant emergency preparedness working groups. Establish liaisons and regular communications with Federal, State, and local-level emergency preparedness working groups, as well as private sector/industry working groups, to share expertise and prepare to better respond to emergencies.

title: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
source_ref: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

title: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should contain the following elements: a) defined roles and responsibilities for personnel with authority during and following an incident; b) a process for activating the response; c) details to manage the immediate consequences of a disruptive incident (with regard to the welfare of individuals; strategic, tactical, and operational response options, and prevention of further loss or unavailability of prioritized activities); d) details on how and under what circumstances the facility will communicate with employees and their relatives, key interested parties, and emergency contacts; e) how the facility will continue or recover its prioritized activities within predetermined timeframes; f) details of the facility's media response following an incident; and g) a process for standing down once the incident is over. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
source_ref: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

title: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should identify stakeholders that need to be notified; critical and time-sensitive applications; alternative work sites; vital records, contact lists, processes, and functions that must be maintained; and personnel, procedures, and resources that are necessary while the facility recovers. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.9, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: For government agencies, a continuity of operations plan may be preferred to a business continuity plan. Develop a continuity of operations plan to document the overarching strategy, policies, and procedures required to support the organization's continuity of operations program. Visit the FEMA Website for related resources, at http://www.fema.gov/planning-templates.

title: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Refer to the Business Continuity Planning Suite, developed by DHS’ National Protection and Programs Directorate and FEMA, for assistance with business continuity planning. This tool is scalable for use by organizations of any size and consists of a business continuity plan training; automated business continuity plan and disaster recovery plan generators; and a self-directed exercise for testing an implemented business continuity plan. The Business Continuity Planning Suite is available at http://www.ready.gov/business-continuity-planning-suite.

title: The corporate-level business continuity plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
vulnerability: The corporate-level business continuity plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
option_for_consideration: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate business continuity plan. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.

title: The corporate-level business continuity plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
vulnerability: The corporate-level business continuity plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan specific to the facility. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.

title: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's business continuity plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of business continuity concerns.
vulnerability: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's business continuity plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of business continuity concerns.
option_for_consideration: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the business continuity plan. The successful establishment of business continuity management is dependent upon its integration with strategic and day-to-day facility management and its alignment with business priorities. To be effective, business continuity management must be recognized from the outset by senior management. Business continuity management must always start at the top – for the essential reason that business continuity management is about what’s important and time-sensitive.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5, p. 13, section 6.7, p. 20; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 5.2, p.10.

title: The facility has not coordinated the business continuity plan with stakeholders.
vulnerability: The facility has not coordinated the business continuity plan with stakeholders.
option_for_consideration: Coordinate the business continuity plan with stakeholders (e.g., customers, regulatory agencies).
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.7.4, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility has not coordinated the business continuity plan with stakeholders.
vulnerability: The facility has not coordinated the business continuity plan with stakeholders.
option_for_consideration: When determining appropriate business continuity management strategies, consider and protect the interests of key stakeholders. Identify appropriate strategies to manage relationships with key stakeholders, business or service partners, and contractors.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25.

title: The facility does not review the business continuity plan annually with stakeholders.
vulnerability: The facility does not review the business continuity plan annually with stakeholders.
option_for_consideration: Review the business continuity plan annually with stakeholders to ensure that business continuity management and the related business continuity plan provide an effective response that safeguards the interests of the facility's stakeholders.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.8, p. 25.

title: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the business continuity plan.
vulnerability: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have a copy of, the business continuity plan.
source_ref: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

title: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the business continuity plan.
vulnerability: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Ensure that key personnel are aware of the business continuity plan, know their contributions to its effectiveness, the implications of not conforming, and their own role during disruptive incidents.
source_ref: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Train all personnel on the business continuity plan annually. Document the completion of training.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.3, p. 14, section 9.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 7.3, p. 13.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: Provide training to the business continuity team on the roles and responsibilities as defined in the business continuity plan. Provide additional training for leaders including incident management.
source_ref: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the business continuity plan.
option_for_consideration: For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.

title: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Exercise and test the business continuity plan annually to ensure procedures are consistent with business continuity objectives. Use appropriate scenarios, and involve relevant interested parties. Produce post-exercise reports containing outcomes, recommendations, and actions to implement improvements.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19.

title: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the business continuity plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; and 8) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective business continuity and recovery.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not document the results of business continuity plan exercises.
vulnerability: The facility does not document the results of business continuity plan exercises.
option_for_consideration: Document the results of business continuity plan exercises. Produce post-exercise reports containing outcomes, recommendations, and actions to implement improvements.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3, p. 36; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19.

vulnerability: The facility's business continuity plan does not identify critical processes and assets necessary for core operations.
option_for_consideration: Identify critical processes and assets required to maintain continuity of operations.
source_ref: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.2.2, p. 15; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.5.3, p. 1600-15, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility's business continuity plan does not identify critical processes and assets necessary for core operations.

option_for_consideration: Identify critical processes and assets necessary for core operations to ensure that mission-critical activities are maintained during a disruptive event.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 8.3.4, p. 29.

title: The facility does not have an alternate site for continuity of business when the primary location is not accessible.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an alternate site for continuity of business when the primary location is not accessible.
option_for_consideration: Establish an alternate site to continue business when the primary location is not accessible. Ensure there is sufficient distance between the alternate site and the facility so they are not likely to be affected by the same incident. Identify the location of the alternate site in the business continuity plan and define who should report to the site and under what conditions.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22.

title: The facility does not have a designated emergency manager. The lack of a designated emergency manager may hinder the implementation of emergency management policies, programs, or procedures.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a designated emergency manager. The lack of a designated emergency manager may hinder the implementation of emergency management policies, programs, or procedures.
option_for_consideration: Designate an employee as emergency manager to develop, implement, and coordinate all emergency management-related activities.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 4.2, p. 1600-6, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 16.

title: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive emergency action plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 5.

title: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive emergency operation/emergency action plan specific to the facility. The emergency operation/action plan should assign responsibilities for carrying out specific actions to protect people (including those with special needs), property, operations, and the environment in an emergency, and to provide incident stabilization. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.8, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
vulnerability: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
option_for_consideration: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate emergency operation/emergency action plan. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.

title: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
vulnerability: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
option_for_consideration: Develop a comprehensive emergency operation/emergency action plan specific to the facility. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.

title: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's emergency operation/emergency action plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of emergency concerns.
vulnerability: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's emergency operation/emergency action plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of emergency concerns.
option_for_consideration: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 11.

title: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.
vulnerability: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.
option_for_consideration: Coordinate the emergency operation/emergency action plan with first responders. Establish MOUs/MOAs as necessary.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

title: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.
vulnerability: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.
option_for_consideration: Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, location of emergency equipment, notification and contact lists) with emergency responders. Consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, for more information.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

title: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
vulnerability: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
option_for_consideration: Review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders, to ensure these agencies are familiar with the facility and its emergency response plans, policies, and procedures.

title: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
vulnerability: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
option_for_consideration: Develop and implement procedures to review the emergency operation/emergency action plan, especially any revisions that have been implemented within the past year, with emergency responders on an annual basis.

title: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
vulnerability: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
option_for_consideration: Provide a copy of the emergency operation/emergency action to the appropriate emergency response agencies.

title: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: Key personnel are not aware of, or do not have access to, the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Ensure key personnel are aware of, and have a copy of, the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 16.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Train all personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan at least annually. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.0, p. 19, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 7, p. 1600-9 to 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Train all personnel on emergency procedures for life safety (e.g., evacuation, shelter, shelter-in-place and lockdown). Train members of the emergency response team so they are familiar with their role and responsibilities as defined within the emergency operation/emergency action plan. Provide advanced training to team leaders, including incident command system training, so they can effectively lead their teams. Maintain records documenting the scope of training, participants, instructors, and duration.
source_ref: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Provide training to obtain and maintain certifications to those emergency response team members that administer First Aid or CPR, or use AEDs.
source_ref: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Provide training in accordance with the applicable OSHA regulation for those employees who use portable fire extinguishers, fire hoses, or other firefighting equipment.
source_ref: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
option_for_consideration: Provide appropriate training to employees who respond to HAZMAT spills.
source_ref: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the emergency action/emergency operations plan.
vulnerability: The facility trains some, but not all, personnel on the emergency action/emergency operations plan.
option_for_consideration: Train all personnel on emergency procedures for life safety at least once a year.

title: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Conduct regular drills and exercises to validate the emergency operation/emergency action plan and to evaluate the ability of personnel to carry out their assigned roles and responsibilities. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/testing/exercises.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Review protective actions for life safety and conduct evacuation drills. Conduct sheltering and lockdown drills.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Evaluate the emergency operation/emergency action plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; 8) Increase awareness and understanding of hazards and the potential impacts of hazards on the facility; and 9) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective response.
source_ref: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Document all exercise results. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and whether the emergency response management system will function as anticipated in the case of a real crisis. Modify the emergency operation/emergency action plan as necessary.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.5.3, p. 37, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

title: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its emergency operation/emergency action plan annually.
option_for_consideration: Consult the Homeland Security Exercise and Evaluation Program Website, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/HSEEP, for a set of fundamental principles that frame a common approach to exercises, among other helpful resources.

title: The facility does not document the results of emergency operation/emergency action plan exercises.
vulnerability: The facility does not document the results of emergency operation/emergency action plan exercises.
option_for_consideration: Document the results of emergency operation/emergency action plan exercises. Implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
source_ref: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3.2, p. 36.

title: The facility does not exchange information with a State or local EOC.
vulnerability: The facility does not exchange information with a State or local EOC.
option_for_consideration: Collaborate with a State and/or local EOC  to facilitate the exchange of real-time situational awareness information.

title: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
option_for_consideration: Establish an IMCC dedicated to facilitating the coordination and support of emergency management activities.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.7.1.1, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 20.

title: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
option_for_consideration: Implement access control measures for the IMCC. Provide members of all teams who staff the IMCC with 24/7 access.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

title: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
option_for_consideration: Ensure the IMCC has an uninterruptible power supply; essential computer, telecommunications, heating/ventilating/air conditioning systems; and other support systems to continue operations for at least 72 hours. In addition, identify and store emergency supplies in the IMCC.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

title: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
option_for_consideration: Determine the feasibility of including the following elements as part of the IMCC: sleeping quarters, dining/food preparation space, portable restrooms, briefing areas, communication area, and proper equipment and backup. Include these elements as appropriate. Ensure there is adequate parking.
source_ref: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 21.

title: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
option_for_consideration: Locate the IMCC offsite to ensure emergency management facilities are available and not impacted by an event at the facility.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, "Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009)," American National Standard, section A.4.7 (m) (iii), p. 33,  http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014. ; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility's IMCC is located onsite. An event that impacts the facility may also make the IMCC unavailable for crisis management.
vulnerability: The facility's IMCC is located onsite. An event that impacts the facility may also make the IMCC unavailable for crisis management.
option_for_consideration: Relocate the IMCC to an offsite location to ensure emergency management facilities are available and not impacted by an event at the facility.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The primary IMCC has not been activated in the previous year through an exercise or an event. Annual activation of the IMCC would allow the facility to evaluate its ability to respond rapidly and effectively to emergencies and identify gaps in planning, training, and resources.
vulnerability: The primary IMCC has not been activated in the previous year through an exercise or an event. Annual activation of the IMCC would allow the facility to evaluate its ability to respond rapidly and effectively to emergencies and identify gaps in planning, training, and resources.
option_for_consideration: Activate the IMCC annually, and complete an after-action report following the activation.

title: The facility did not complete an after-action report following the activation of its IMCC.
vulnerability: The facility did not complete an after-action report following the activation of its IMCC.
option_for_consideration: Complete an after-action report following each activation of the IMCC and implement procedures for capturing and implementing lessons learned.

title: The IMCC cannot operate independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.
vulnerability: The IMCC cannot operate independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.
option_for_consideration: Investigate ways to make the IMCC capable of operating independently of all outside utilities for at least 72 hours.
source_ref: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section A.6.7.1.1, p. 1600-20, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

title: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
vulnerability: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
option_for_consideration: Incorporate a threat level system into the security plan.
source_ref: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

title: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
vulnerability: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
option_for_consideration: Incorporate the DHS NTAS into the security plan. The NTAS provides information timely, detailed information about terrorist threats to the public, government agencies, first responders, airports and other transportation hubs, and the private sector. Consult the DHS NTAS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/national-terrorism-advisory-system, for more information.

title: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
vulnerability: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
option_for_consideration: Incorporate MARSEC levels into the security plan. MARSEC is a three-tiered system employed by the USGS to communicate with maritime industry partners of potential pre-planned responses to threats. MARSEC levels adjust in conjunction with NTAS alerts. Consult the USGS MARSEC Levels Website, at http://www.uscg.mil/safetylevels/whatismarsec.asp, for more information.

title: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
vulnerability: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
option_for_consideration: Incorporate an industry threat level system into the security plan.

title: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.
vulnerability: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.
option_for_consideration: Explore the feasibility of employing and implementing additional protective measures to increase the overall security posture of the facility as threat levels increase. Options may include additional access controls; barriers; increased communications and notification; enhanced cyber security; additional infrastructure upgrades and redundancy; enhanced incident response; additional monitoring and surveillance detection; and planning and preparedness.

title: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.
vulnerability: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.
option_for_consideration: Conduct planning and coordination and gather resources to implement additional protective measures appropriate for the facility during elevated threat situations in a timely manner. Train facility personnel on their responsibilities associated with these protective measures, and exercise these measures annually to evaluate facility preparedness and program effectiveness.
